topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official email felt like a physical blow, landing squarely in my inbox at 10 AM. Dr. Albright was departing. My carefully constructed academic scaffolding, the entire direction of my final year project, was suddenly unmoored. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless pixels. My hands, which had been gripping my coffee mug so tightly I could feel the ceramic digging into my palms, slowly uncurled. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, a cold, heavy weight that made drawing a full breath feel like an effort. I remembered the countless late nights spent poring over his obscure theories, the way his encouragement had felt like a lifeline. Now, that lifeline was being cut. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and let out a slow, shaky exhale. The silence of my dorm room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I picked up my pen, intending to start drafting a new outline, but the paper seemed alien, the words refusing to form into anything coherent.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened windowpane of the library. My face looked pale, the shadows under my eyes deep enough to be chasms. When I finally read the announcement about Professor Davies’ departure, the sound seemed to drain from the room. It wasn't just a professional loss; it was the abrupt evaporation of the structure that had been supporting my entire academic ambition. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing difficult. I traced the condensation on the glass with a fingertip, the cold seeping into my skin. I replayed past conversations in my head—the moments I had been too hesitant to ask the clarifying questions, the times I had let my own insecurities overshadow the intellectual pursuit. A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I pushed away from my desk, the movement stiff, and walked toward the exit, needing the sharp, indifferent air to clear the fog in my mind.

[story 3]

The news hit me during lunch break, a sudden, sharp jolt that made my fork clatter onto my tray. I watched the notification pop up, and for a moment, the bustling cafeteria noise seemed to warp, becoming distant and muffled. My stomach clenched into a tight knot. I looked around at the other students, laughing and discussing weekend plans, and felt utterly isolated in my distress. I picked at the crust of my sandwich, the texture suddenly repulsive. I thought about how much I had relied on Dr. Evans’ steady guidance, how I had let my own fear of failure keep me from pushing myself further. A profound sense of having wasted potential settled over me, a dull throb behind my eyes. I slowly pushed my tray away, the clatter echoing too loudly in the sudden quiet of my internal world. I didn't move to call anyone; I just stood there, feeling the weight of the impending void.
